title: Safe Medication Administration: The Rights and Three Checks
subtitle: The nurse gives medications safely — the rights of administration, three label checks, two identifiers, checking allergies, staying until the dose is taken, and clarifying unclear orders.

patient.name: Medication Safety
patient.admitDate: The nurse administers medications safely using the rights, three checks, and two identifiers.

nursesNote: The nurse administers medications safely using the rights of administration — right patient, drug, dose, route, and time, plus right reason, documentation, response, and the patient's right to refuse. The label is checked three times: when taking it from storage, when preparing it, and at the bedside before giving it. The patient is verified with two identifiers (name and date of birth or medical record number, not the room number), and allergies are checked first. The nurse gives only medications they personally prepared, does not leave doses at the bedside, and stays until the patient takes the medication. Extended-release and enteric-coated tablets are not crushed; a liquid form is used when needed. Any unclear, illegible, or unsafe order is held and clarified with the prescriber before administration, high-alert drugs receive an independent double-check, and documentation is done after the dose is given. Medication errors are reported and the patient is monitored.
providerOrders: Apply the rights of medication administration for every dose
Perform three label checks and verify two patient identifiers
Check allergies before administering any medication
Hold and clarify any unclear, illegible, or unsafe order
Document after administration and report any error

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about safe medication administration?

opts.text: The rights include right patient, drug, dose, route, and time
Two identifiers are used to verify the patient
The medication label is checked three times
A patient can be identified by room number alone
Allergies are checked before giving a medication
The nurse stays until the patient takes the medication
The nurse should not give a medication prepared by someone else
An unclear or illegible order is clarified before administering
Extended-release and enteric-coated tablets should not be crushed
Documentation should be done before the medication is given
High-alert medications often require an independent double-check
The patient has the right to refuse a medication
Verify the order and check for allergies
Perform the label checks while preparing the medication
Identify the patient with two identifiers
Administer and stay until it is taken
Document the medication after it is given
Checking two identifiers before giving a medication
Identifying the patient by room number
Performing three label checks
Giving a medication someone else prepared
Staying until the patient takes the medication
Leaving pills at the bedside for later
Checking allergies before administration
Crushing an extended-release tablet to ease swallowing

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A medication order is illegible and unclear
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Do not give the medication
Clarify the order with the prescriber
Verify the correct drug, dose, and route
Administer once the order is clarified
Document the clarification
Verifying the patient with name and date of birth
Administering a drug despite an unclear order
Performing an independent double-check on a high-alert drug
Charting a medication before giving it
Honoring the patient's right to refuse
Leaving the medication for the patient to take later
Reporting a medication error and monitoring the patient
Giving a dose another nurse poured
